examples.title: FCFS Disk Scheduling
examples.description: First Come First Serve disk scheduling algorithm implementation
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FCFS_Scheduling(requests[], start_head)
BEGIN
  total_seek = 0
  current_head = start_head
  
  PRINT "FCFS Disk Scheduling"
  PRINT "Initial head position:", start_head
  
  FOR i = 0 TO requests.length-1 DO
    seek_distance = ABS(requests[i] - current_head)
    total_seek = total_seek + seek_distance
    PRINT "Move from", current_head, "to", requests[i], 
          "- Distance:", seek_distance
    current_head = requests[i]
  END FOR
  
  PRINT "Total seek distance:", total_seek
  RETURN total_seek
END
examples.explanation: FCFS services requests in the order they arrived. It is simple but does not provide optimal seek time. This implementation calculates the total head movement required to service all requests in arrival order.

examples.title: SSTF Disk Scheduling
examples.description: Shortest Seek Time First disk scheduling algorithm in Python
examples.code:
def sstf_scheduling(requests, start_head):
    """
    SSTF: Service request closest to current head position
    """
    total_seek = 0
    current_head = start_head
    remaining = requests.copy()
    
    print("SSTF Disk Scheduling")
    print(f"Initial head position: {start_head}")
    
    while remaining:
        # Find closest request to current head
        closest = min(remaining, key=lambda x: abs(x - current_head))
        seek_distance = abs(closest - current_head)
        total_seek += seek_distance
        
        print(f"Move from {current_head} to {closest} - Distance: {seek_distance}")
        
        current_head = closest
        remaining.remove(closest)
    
    print(f"Total seek distance: {total_seek}")
    return total_seek

# Example usage
requests = [95, 180, 34, 119, 11, 45, 62, 24]
start_head = 50
sstf_scheduling(requests, start_head)
examples.explanation: SSTF services the request closest to the current head position, reducing total seek time compared to FCFS. However, it may cause starvation for requests far from the current position.

examples.title: SCAN Disk Scheduling (Elevator Algorithm)
examples.description: SCAN algorithm implementation that moves like an elevator
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SCAN_Scheduling(requests[], start_head, max_cylinder)
BEGIN
  total_seek = 0
  current_head = start_head
  direction = 1  // 1 = moving toward higher numbers
  
  // Sort requests in ascending order
  sorted_requests = SORT(requests)
  
  PRINT "SCAN Disk Scheduling (Elevator Algorithm)"
  PRINT "Initial head position:", start_head
  
  // Find split point based on current head position
  split = 0
  FOR i = 0 TO sorted_requests.length-1 DO
    IF sorted_requests[i] >= current_head THEN
      split = i
      BREAK
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // Service requests in forward direction
  FOR i = split TO sorted_requests.length-1 DO
    seek_distance = ABS(sorted_requests[i] - current_head)
    total_seek = total_seek + seek_distance
    PRINT "Move from", current_head, "to", sorted_requests[i]
    current_head = sorted_requests[i]
  END FOR
  
  // Go to end (max cylinder)
  IF current_head < max_cylinder THEN
    total_seek = total_seek + (max_cylinder - current_head)
    current_head = max_cylinder
  END IF
  
  // Reverse and service remaining requests
  FOR i = split-1 TO 0 DO
    seek_distance = ABS(sorted_requests[i] - current_head)
    total_seek = total_seek + seek_distance
    PRINT "Move from", current_head, "to", sorted_requests[i]
    current_head = sorted_requests[i]
  END FOR
  
  PRINT "Total seek distance:", total_seek
  RETURN total_seek
END
examples.explanation: SCAN algorithm moves the head in one direction servicing all requests until it reaches the end, then reverses direction. Like an elevator, it services all requests on its way before changing direction. This provides better performance than FCFS while avoiding starvation.

examples.title: Disk Access Time Calculator
examples.description: C program to calculate disk access time components
examples.explanation: This program calculates the total disk access time by combining seek time, average rotational latency, transfer time, and controller overhead. It demonstrates the mathematical formulas used in disk I/O performance analysis.
